papers.title: Microbiota-mediated induction of beige adipocytes in response to dietary cues.
papers.abstract: Interactions between diet and the gut microbiota are fundamental to metabolic health, shaping energy balance and disease susceptibility1-5. However, the underlying mechanisms by which dietary and microbial factors converge to regulate host physiology remain unclear. Here we show that protein availability profoundly modulates the functional landscape of the gut microbiota and promotes remodelling of white adipose tissue (WAT). Specifically, low-protein diets (LPDs) robustly induce signature genes
papers.sonnetReason: Low-protein diet driving gut microbiota remodeling and beige adipocyte induction is highly relevant to Chris's dietary protein restriction work, and the microbiota mechanism is a genuinely surprising novel angle he may not have anticipated.
papers.aiSummary: Low-protein diets reshape the gut microbiota in ways that promote browning/beiging of white adipose tissue. The study identifies a mechanistic link between dietary protein availability, microbial community function, and adipose tissue remodeling.
papers.whyItMatters: Why this matters to you: Directly relevant — low-protein diets are a core tool in your work, and this paper identifies a microbiota-WAT beiging axis downstream of protein restriction that could interact with or be independent of FGF21-mediated adipose remodeling you study.

papers.title: Central FGF19 reduces hypothalamic inflammation and improves leptin signaling in obese mice.
papers.abstract: Obesity is a chronic metabolic disorder characterized by systemic and hypothalamic inflammation that impairs neuronal signaling and disrupts the fine-tuning of energy balance regulation. Leptin resistance, a hallmark of obesity, arises from this inflammatory state and contributes to increased food intake and reduced energy expenditure (EE). Fibroblast growth factor 19 (FGF19), a gut-derived hormone with beneficial metabolic effects in peripheral tissues, also acts centrally to reduce body weight
papers.sonnetReason: Central FGF19 (closely related to FGF21) acting in hypothalamus to reduce inflammation and improve leptin signaling is highly relevant to Chris's FGF family and CNS energy balance interests.
papers.aiSummary: Central administration of FGF19 reduced hypothalamic inflammation and restored leptin sensitivity in obese mice, leading to decreased food intake and improved energy balance. The findings suggest FGF19 acts in the brain to counteract obesity-associated neuroinflammation.
papers.whyItMatters: Why this matters to you: Directly relevant — FGF19 is the human ortholog of FGF15 and shares receptor biology with FGF21; this paper's demonstration of central FGF19 action on hypothalamic inflammation and leptin signaling is a close parallel to your CNS-FGF21 work and raises questions about shared vs. distinct central mechanisms.

papers.title: Brainstem GLP-1 neurons modulate physiological satiation and drive sustained weight loss in obese mice.
papers.abstract: Glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor (GLP-1R) activation in the brain strongly reduces appetite, but most brain GLP-1Rs are not accessible for systemically administered GLP-1R agonists. Acute activation of nucleus tractus solitarius (NTS) GLP-1 neurons, known as preproglucagon (PPG) neurons, strongly suppresses food intake separate from GLP-1R agonists. However, it is unknown if chronic stimulation of PPG neurons is a viable strategy for appetite suppression, or if obesity disrupts their function. H
papers.sonnetReason: Directly relevant to CNS GLP-1 mechanisms and NTS/brainstem circuits for appetite suppression, core areas of Chris's interest in central incretin signaling.
papers.aiSummary: Chronic chemogenetic activation of brainstem NTS preproglucagon neurons suppressed appetite and produced sustained weight loss in obese mice without apparent tolerance or disrupted function. This establishes PPG neurons as a viable long-term target for appetite suppression distinct from systemic GLP-1R agonist access.
papers.whyItMatters: Why this matters to you: Indirectly relevant — this is a brainstem satiety circuit paper with limited direct FGF21/protein restriction connection, but the chronic CNS activation strategy and obesity-resilience of the circuit may inform how you think about sustained central FGF21 signaling effects.

papers.title: A spatial and projection-based transcriptomic atlas of paraventricular hypothalamic cell types.
papers.abstract: The paraventricular hypothalamus (PVH) controls behavioral and physiologic processes, including appetite, social behavior, autonomic outflow, and pituitary hormone secretion. However, molecular markers for centrally projecting PVH neuron populations remain largely undefined, and a complete census of PVH cell types has not been established. Therefore, we performed extensive single-cell/nucleus RNA sequencing to catalog PVH neuron subtypes and multiplexed error-robust fluorescence in situ hybridiz
papers.sonnetReason: A comprehensive transcriptomic atlas of PVH cell types and projection neurons is highly useful for Chris's work on hypothalamic circuits controlling appetite and energy balance.
papers.aiSummary: Single-cell/nucleus RNA sequencing and spatial transcriptomics were used to build a comprehensive atlas of PVH neuron subtypes, including previously undefined projection-specific populations. The study provides molecular markers for centrally projecting PVH neurons that control appetite, autonomic outflow, and pituitary function.
papers.whyItMatters: Why this matters to you: Relevant as a resource — the PVH is a key node downstream of FGF21 CNS action, and this atlas could help you identify specific cell types mediating FGF21's effects on energy expenditure and macronutrient preference.

papers.title: FAM237B, a conserved orexigenic neuropeptide, is regulated by fasting, insulin, and neuroinflammation in mouse hypothalamic NPY/AgRP neurons.
papers.abstract: Agouti-related peptide (AgRP) and neuropeptide Y (NPY) neurons in the arcuate nucleus integrate metabolic and inflammatory signals to control food intake. FAM237B (Gm8773/NPGM) is a putative orexigenic peptide enriched in a subset of AgRP neurons, yet misannotated as a long non-coding RNA in mice that has since been recognized as a 139 aa microprotein. Here, we combine evolutionary, transcriptomic, and physiological approaches to define FAM237B as an ancient, metabolically regulated neuropeptide
papers.sonnetReason: Novel orexigenic microprotein in arcuate AgRP/NPY neurons is directly relevant to hypothalamic feeding circuits Chris studies, and the novel peptide angle adds some surprise.
papers.aiSummary: FAM237B is a conserved orexigenic microprotein expressed in a subset of AgRP neurons that is upregulated by fasting and suppressed by insulin and neuroinflammation. It functions as a bona fide metabolically regulated neuropeptide rather than a non-coding RNA, adding complexity to AgRP neuron signaling.
papers.whyItMatters: Why this matters to you: Indirectly relevant — AgRP neurons are key sensors of protein/amino acid status and are regulated by FGF21 signaling; FAM237B's regulation by fasting and metabolic state in this circuit could intersect with protein-restriction-driven neuroendocrine responses you study.

papers.title: Metabolic regulation by tanycyte-derived extracellular vesicles through insulin precursor-mediated neuronal recognition and mTORC component delivery
papers.abstract: Nature Metabolism, Published online: 05 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s42255-026-01474-3 Tanycyte-derived extracellular vesicles are shown to regulate feeding diurnality and metabolic balance through insulin precursor-mediated targeting and delivery of mTORC components to hypothalamic neurons.
papers.sonnetReason: Mechanistic detail on tanycyte extracellular vesicles delivering mTORC components to hypothalamic neurons is a genuinely novel hypothalamic signaling mechanism with surprise value.
papers.aiSummary: Extracellular vesicles released by hypothalamic α-tanycytes carry mTORC components and use an insulin precursor as a targeting signal to deliver cargo to specific hypothalamic neurons, regulating diurnal feeding patterns and metabolic homeostasis. This reveals a non-synaptic, vesicle-based communication mechanism within the hypothalamus.
papers.whyItMatters: Why this matters to you: Indirectly relevant — tanycytes are implicated in hypothalamic nutrient sensing and FGF21 receptor expression; this novel vesicle-based signaling mechanism could represent an underappreciated layer of CNS metabolic regulation operating in the same circuits you study.

papers.title: Survodutide acts through circumventricular organs in the brain and activates neuronal regions associated with appetite regulation.
papers.abstract: Survodutide is a novel GCG/GLP-1 receptor (GCGR/GLP-1R) dual agonist in clinical development for people with obesity and people with metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (MASH). Preclinically, survodutide demonstrated body weight lowering efficacy through decreased energy intake and increased energy expenditure. Here, we investigated the central site of action of survodutide and provide further insights into its mechanism of action in reducing body weight. We assessed GCGR and GLP1R
papers.sonnetReason: Central mechanisms of GCG/GLP-1 dual agonist action through circumventricular organs and appetite-regulating brain regions is relevant to Chris's incretin/CNS interests.
papers.aiSummary: The dual GCG/GLP-1R agonist survodutide reduces body weight primarily by acting on circumventricular organs accessible to circulating peptides, activating downstream appetite-regulatory brain regions. Both receptor types contribute to its central anorectic and energy expenditure effects.
papers.whyItMatters: Why this matters to you: Indirectly relevant — survodutide's CNS mechanism through circumventricular organs is conceptually parallel to how circulating FGF21 accesses the brain, and the dual-receptor energy expenditure component may be worth comparing to FGF21's thermogenic CNS actions.

papers.title: Tiny extracellular vesicles drive big feeding effects
papers.abstract: Nature Metabolism, Published online: 05 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s42255-026-01472-5 A study published in Nature Metabolism shows that extracellular vesicles from a small group of hypothalamic α-tanycytes direct diurnal feeding behaviour to maintain body weight and glucose homeostasis.
papers.sonnetReason: Tanycyte-derived extracellular vesicles regulating diurnal feeding is an unexpected mechanistic angle for hypothalamic energy balance control that Chris may not have encountered.
papers.aiSummary: This is a News & Views commentary summarizing the tanycyte extracellular vesicle paper (paper 6), highlighting that a small population of α-tanycytes uses vesicles to control diurnal feeding and glucose homeostasis. It contextualizes the mechanistic novelty of tanycyte-to-neuron vesicular communication.
papers.whyItMatters: Why this matters to you: Same indirect relevance as paper 6 — read alongside the primary paper rather than independently; no additional new data.

papers.title: GCN2 in proteostasis: structural logic, signalling networks and disease.
papers.abstract: Proteostasis is the finely tuned balance of protein synthesis, folding and degradation essential for cellular health. When this equilibrium is disrupted, misfolded proteins accumulate, triggering adaptive stress responses such as the unfolded protein response and the integrated stress response (ISR). Central to the ISR is the kinase GCN2, a sensor of amino acid deprivation and ribosomal stress. Upon activation, GCN2 phosphorylates eIF2α, dampening global translation while selectively enhan
papers.sonnetReason: GCN2 as an amino acid deprivation sensor is directly relevant to the dietary protein restriction signaling pathway upstream of FGF21, though this review focuses on proteostasis/disease rather than metabolic/CNS context.
papers.aiSummary: This review covers GCN2, the kinase that senses amino acid deprivation and phosphorylates eIF2α to activate the integrated stress response, detailing its structural logic, signaling network, and roles in disease. GCN2 is positioned as a master regulator of cellular proteostasis under nutrient stress.
papers.whyItMatters: Why this matters to you: Directly relevant — GCN2 is a key sensor of dietary amino acid insufficiency and an upstream activator of FGF21 transcription during protein restriction; this review is a useful mechanistic reference for the sensing arm of your protein-restriction/FGF21 axis.

papers.title: Thermal-state-dependent control of body temperature and feeding by two intra-hypothalamic pathways.
papers.abstract: The intricate interplay between energy metabolism and body temperature regulation underscores the necessity of finely tuned mechanisms to maintain thermo-energetic homeostasis. Hot environments are known to suppress food intake and to reduce energy expenditure. However, the interplay between thermoregulatory and caloric-regulatory hypothalamic areas remains largely unexplored. In this study, we unveil two pathways originating from a subpopulation of genetically defined leptin receptor-expressing
papers.sonnetReason: Hypothalamic leptin receptor circuits linking thermoregulation and feeding are relevant to Chris's energy expenditure and hypothalamic neuroscience interests but not a primary focus.
papers.aiSummary: Two distinct intra-hypothalamic pathways from leptin receptor-expressing neurons were found to separately control feeding suppression and reduced energy expenditure in response to warm ambient temperatures. The study dissociates the thermoregulatory and caloric components of heat-induced hypophagia.
papers.whyItMatters: Why this matters to you: Indirectly relevant — FGF21 influences both thermogenesis and food intake via hypothalamic circuits, and understanding how leptin receptor neurons coordinate temperature and feeding could inform interpretation of FGF21's overlapping effects on these same outputs.

papers.title: Ectopic, hepatic GLP-1R agonism enhances the weight loss efficacy of GLP-1 analogues.
papers.abstract: Unimolecular triagonists drive substantial weight loss in patients with obesity by engaging the glucagon-like peptide 1 receptor (GLP-1R) and glucose dependent insulinotropic polypeptide receptor (GIPR) to reduce food intake (FI) and the hepatic glucagon receptor (GcgR) to enhance energy expenditure (EE). However, their development has been challenged by deleterious cardiovascular (CV) effects, including increased heart rate (HR), elongated QTc, and arrhythmia mediated by GcgR agonism. GLP-1R mo
papers.sonnetReason: Hepatic GLP-1R agonism and triagonist mechanisms touch on Chris's GLP-1 and energy expenditure interests but the cardiovascular pharmacology angle is not his primary domain.
papers.aiSummary: This study explores whether adding hepatic GLP-1R agonism to existing GLP-1 analogues can boost weight loss without the cardiovascular side effects seen with glucagon receptor agonism in triagonists. The approach appears to enhance energy expenditure and food intake reduction through liver-specific GLP-1R signaling, potentially offering a safer alternative to GcgR-based strategies.
papers.whyItMatters: Why this matters to you: Indirectly relevant—FGF21 is a key hepatokine induced downstream of GLP-1/glucagon signaling, and understanding which receptor arms drive weight loss versus cardiovascular risk could inform how FGF21 fits mechanistically into multi-agonist therapy frameworks.

papers.title: FGF23: A player not only in bone diseases.
papers.abstract: The skeleton is able to secrete hormones and mediate endocrine functions. Particularly, the fibroblast growth factor 23 (FGF23) has received considerable attention for its unique phosphaturic properties, raising the bone to the status of an essential endocrine regulator. Excess in circulating levels of FGF23, not so surprisingly, then associated to mineral imbalance, in hereditary/acquired mineral and skeletal disorders. A key player, FGF23 excess precedes mineral and skeletal disturbances. Howe
papers.sonnetReason: FGF23 as an endocrine hormone beyond bone disease is relevant to Chris's interest in FGF family members (FGF19, FGF21, FGF23) in metabolic contexts, though the focus remains largely on mineral metabolism.
papers.aiSummary: This review positions FGF23 as a broader endocrine hormone beyond its phosphaturic role in bone, linking excess circulating FGF23 to cardiovascular, renal, and metabolic consequences that precede overt mineral disorders. It highlights FGF23 as an early biomarker and potential causal factor in systemic disease beyond skeletal pathology.
papers.whyItMatters: Why this matters to you: This is only tangentially relevant to your work—while FGF23 is a family member of FGF21 and both use Klotho co-receptors, FGF23's biology is largely phosphate/kidney-focused and distant from FGF21's role in protein restriction and CNS metabolism regulation.

papers.title: Central activation of chaperone-mediated autophagy reduces appetite by fine-tuning hypothalamic amino acid pools: new insights from fish.
papers.journal: American journal of physiology. Cell physiology
papers.abstract: Chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA) is a key lysosomal proteolytic pathway essential for cellular homeostasis and metabolism, with dysfunction linked to various human diseases. Although extensively studied in humans and mice, CMA was only recently identified in fish, paving the way for novel and evolutionary research perspectives. Here, we demonstrate a role for CMA in regulating feed intake (FI) in rainbow trout (Oncorhynchus mykiss), a major aquaculture species and a widely used model in numero
papers.sonnetReason: Hypothalamic amino acid sensing to regulate feeding connects to Chris's protein restriction work, but the fish model and CMA focus make this somewhat peripheral.
papers.aiSummary: This study shows that central activation of chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA) in rainbow trout suppresses food intake by fine-tuning hypothalamic amino acid pools, suggesting CMA acts as a nutrient-sensing mechanism in the brain. This is the first demonstration of CMA regulating feeding behavior through hypothalamic amino acid signaling in a non-mammalian vertebrate.
papers.whyItMatters: Why this matters to you: Directly relevant—this paper links hypothalamic amino acid sensing to appetite suppression, which intersects closely with your interest in how the CNS detects and responds to dietary protein restriction, potentially involving FGF21-dependent and independent pathways.

papers.title: Hepatic steatosis in humans is associated with preserved glucagon action on amino acid metabolism
papers.abstract: BACKGROUND Amino acid (AA) concentrations are increased in prediabetes and diabetes. Since AAs stimulate glucagon secretion, which should then increase hepatic AA catabolism, it has been hypothesized that hepatic resistance (associated with hepatic fat content) to glucagon’s actions on AA metabolism leads to hyperglucagonemia and hyperglycemia.METHODS To test this hypothesis, we therefore studied lean and obese individuals, the latter group with and without hepatic steatosis as defined by proton
papers.sonnetReason: Amino acid metabolism and glucagon signaling in hepatic steatosis touches on AA sensing relevant to protein restriction work, but the hepatic glucagon resistance focus is somewhat peripheral.
papers.aiSummary: Contrary to the prevailing hypothesis, this human study found that hepatic steatosis does not impair glucagon's ability to stimulate amino acid catabolism, suggesting that hyperglucagonemia in obese/steatotic individuals is not driven by hepatic glucagon resistance to amino acid metabolism. Instead, elevated amino acids in these patients may reflect increased amino acid flux from other sources rather than defective hepatic clearance.
papers.whyItMatters: Why this matters to you: Relevant to your work—glucagon and FGF21 are co-regulated in the context of protein/amino acid metabolism and hepatic nutrient sensing, and this finding challenges assumptions about how amino acid signals drive hormonal responses, which could reframe how FGF21 fits into this axis.

papers.title: The S100A8/A9 complex promotes food intake and prevents adipose tissue loss during cancer cachexia in mice.
papers.abstract: Cancer cachexia is a wasting syndrome characterized by reduced food intake and lean and fat tissue loss. In mice, cancer cachexia involved marked reductions in host fat and lean mass (particularly skeletal muscle), which were balanced by tumor growth. Using 15N tracing, the tumor gets protein (nitrogen) from both food intake and host tissue breakdown. Total energy expenditure remained unchanged due to metabolic compensation among the tumor, brown adipose tissue (BAT), and other organs, a phenome
papers.sonnetReason: Cancer cachexia involving food intake regulation, adipose/muscle wasting, and energy expenditure compensation is modestly relevant; the S100A8/A9 orexigenic mechanism is an unexpected angle.
papers.aiSummary: The S100A8/A9 alarmin complex was found to stimulate food intake and partially protect adipose tissue during cancer cachexia in mice, acting as a compensatory signal that the tumor co-opts to sustain nitrogen supply from both diet and host tissue. Total energy expenditure was maintained through metabolic redistribution among tumor, BAT, and other organs, revealing unexpected homeostatic compensation during cachexia.
papers.whyItMatters: Why this matters to you: Indirectly relevant—the nitrogen tracing showing protein sourcing from host tissues during cachexia touches on whole-body protein metabolism regulation, but this paper's focus on tumor biology and S100 proteins is fairly distant from FGF21/CNS metabolic regulation.

papers.title: A Large Deletion With a Large Impact: Homozygous 5,600 bp Deletion of the GALNT3 Gene Causing Hyperphosphatemic Tumoral Calcinosis.
papers.abstract: Hyperphosphatemic familial tumoral calcinosis (HTC) is a rare disease caused by autosomal recessive loss of function variants in the genes encoding fibroblast growth factor 23 (FGF-23), Klotho, or GalNAc-T3. This results in reduced phosphate excretion in the renal proximal tubule, leading to hyperphosphatemia. The clinical manifestations of HTC are mainly periarticular calcifications accompanied by pain and disability, inflammation, and dental problems. Inactive forms or reduced levels of FGF-23
papers.sonnetReason: Directly involves FGF23, Klotho, and GALNT3 in phosphate regulation — FGF/Klotho biology is relevant, though renal/bone context is distant from Chris's CNS/feeding focus.
papers.aiSummary: This case report describes a patient with a rare homozygous 5,600 bp deletion in GALNT3, which prevents proper glycosylation and secretion of intact FGF23, resulting in hyperphosphatemic tumoral calcinosis with severe periarticular calcifications. The case illustrates how loss of FGF23 bioactivity—through impaired post-translational processing rather than gene absence—leads to phosphate retention and ectopic calcification.
papers.whyItMatters: Why this matters to you: Not directly relevant to your work—this is a rare monogenic disorder of FGF23 processing affecting phosphate homeostasis, with no clear connection to FGF21, protein restriction, or CNS metabolic regulation.

papers.title: Weighing in on the incretin revolution
papers.abstract: Nature Metabolism, Published online: 04 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s42255-026-01490-3 Incretin-based therapies have revolutionized the treatment of obesity and related metabolic disorders. On World Obesity Day (4 March), we discuss incretin-based therapies as go-to treatments for obesity and its comorbidities, highlighting triumphs and cautionary tales.
papers.sonnetReason: Incretin-based therapies overview in Nature Metabolism is relevant to his GLP-1/GIP interests but is a broad perspective piece rather than mechanistic research.
papers.aiSummary: This perspective piece reviews the clinical successes of incretin-based therapies (GLP-1, GIP, glucagon receptor agonists) for obesity and metabolic disease, while also noting emerging safety concerns and gaps in understanding long-term effects. It contextualizes the incretin revolution within broader obesity pharmacology on World Obesity Day 2026.
papers.whyItMatters: Why this matters to you: Marginally relevant as broad context—FGF21 analogs are emerging in the same therapeutic space as incretins for obesity and metabolic disease, so understanding the clinical landscape and unmet needs of incretin therapy could inform how FGF21-based approaches might be positioned.

papers.title: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis of Semaglutide Effects on Adipose Tissue and Emerging Effects on Brain and Cognition.
papers.journal: Obesity reviews : an official journal of the International Association for the Study of Obesity
papers.abstract: In 2023 and 2024, research into semaglutide (SEMA), an antiobesity and antidiabetic medication, indicated potential benefits beyond its approved uses, particularly in preventing Alzheimer's disease (AD). This highlights the link between obesity and AD development. This systematic review and meta-analysis evaluates clinical studies assessing SEMA's effects on subcutaneous and visceral adipose tissue (SAT/VAT) measures, brain function, cognitive performance through cognitive tasks, and the inciden
papers.sonnetReason: Semaglutide/GLP-1 effects on brain and cognition are tangentially relevant to Chris's interest in central GLP-1 mechanisms, but this is a clinical meta-analysis focused on adipose tissue and Alzheimer's disease rather than CNS feeding circuits.
papers.aiSummary: This systematic review and meta-analysis finds that semaglutide significantly reduces both subcutaneous and visceral adipose tissue and shows emerging evidence for benefits in brain function, cognitive performance, and reduced Alzheimer's disease incidence. The data suggest GLP-1R agonism has CNS effects beyond appetite suppression that may protect against neurodegeneration.
papers.whyItMatters: Why this matters to you: Relevant in a cross-cutting way—semaglutide's emerging CNS and cognitive effects parallel growing interest in FGF21's central actions, and understanding how GLP-1R agonism affects brain metabolism and cognition provides comparative context for evaluating FGF21's central mechanisms.

papers.title: Dapiglutide, a dual GLP-1 and GLP-2 receptor agonist, for obesity: a randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled parallel-group, proof-of-concept trial.
papers.abstract: Dapiglutide, a dual glucagon-like peptide (GLP)-1 and GLP-2 receptor agonist, is under clinical development for bodyweight reduction in obesity, capitalising on GLP-1 receptor-mediated food intake-reducing effect leading to bodyweight loss and GLP-2 receptor-mediated improvement of gut barrier function and anti-inflammatory properties. In this 12-week, investigator-initiated, phase IIa, proof-of-concept, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial in Denmark, adults (18-75 years) with obesity (BMI &#
papers.sonnetReason: Dual GLP-1/GLP-2 agonist for obesity is relevant to Chris's GLP-1 interests but is a clinical trial paper rather than mechanistic work on central/neural pathways.
papers.aiSummary: In a 12-week phase IIa trial, dapiglutide—a dual GLP-1/GLP-2 receptor agonist—produced meaningful weight loss in adults with obesity while also improving gut barrier function and reducing inflammation, consistent with its dual mechanism. This proof-of-concept study supports GLP-2R agonism as a complementary target to GLP-1R for obesity treatment.
papers.whyItMatters: Why this matters to you: Only indirectly relevant—the gut-barrier and anti-inflammatory angles of GLP-2R agonism are mechanistically distant from FGF21 and CNS protein sensing, though the broader theme of gut-derived signals influencing metabolic outcomes provides peripheral context.

papers.title: The role of GIP in carbohydrate metabolism: Implications in the development of therapies for T2DM, a narrative review.
papers.abstract: For the last three decades, bariatric/metabolic surgeries have highlighted the relevance of certain gastrointestinal hormones in controlling and regulating glucose metabolism. The incretins have been a significant focus in developing therapies against Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus (T2DM). Glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) has been a primary focus in this field, leading to the development of analogues with high therapeutic potential and efficiency, such as semaglutide. However, recently another incretin
papers.sonnetReason: GIP biology and incretin therapies are relevant to Chris's incretin interests, but this narrative review of GIP in carbohydrate metabolism is not mechanistically focused on CNS or feeding circuits.
papers.aiSummary: This narrative review covers GIP's role in carbohydrate metabolism and its growing relevance as a therapeutic target for type 2 diabetes, particularly in combination with GLP-1 as in tirzepatide. It summarizes how GIP receptor signaling complements GLP-1 in regulating insulin secretion, fat metabolism, and body weight.
papers.whyItMatters: Why this matters to you: Not directly relevant to your work—GIP biology is centered on incretin/insulin physiology with little connection to FGF21, protein restriction signaling, or CNS metabolic regulation, though the broader incretin field overlaps with the therapeutic space FGF21 analogs occupy.

papers.title: Diet quality and nutritional adequacy during a 2-year calorie restriction intervention: the Comprehensive Assessment of Long-Term Effects of Reducing Intake of Energy 2 trial.
papers.journal: The American journal of clinical nutrition
papers.abstract: Comprehensive Assessment of Long-Term Effects of Reducing Intake of Energy (CALERIE) was the first randomized controlled trial of calorie restriction (CR) on biomarkers of aging and cardiometabolic health in humans without obesity. The aim of this secondary data analysis was to evaluate diet quality and nutritional adequacy during a 2-y CR intervention among healthy adults in the CALERIE trial. CALERIE 2 was a multisite trial of healthy adults randomly assigned to 2 y of 25% CR or an ad libitum
papers.sonnetReason: Caloric restriction in humans is tangentially relevant to Chris's interest in CR and metabolic aging, but this is a diet quality/nutritional adequacy secondary analysis with no mechanistic focus on his core signaling interests.
papers.aiSummary: This secondary analysis of the CALERIE 2 trial examined whether 2 years of 25% calorie restriction compromised diet quality or nutritional adequacy in healthy, non-obese adults. The CR group maintained or improved overall diet quality metrics while achieving meaningful caloric reduction, though some micronutrient intake gaps were observed.
papers.whyItMatters: Why this matters to you: Indirectly relevant — CALERIE 2 is a key human CR dataset, and understanding whether participants were also inadvertently restricting protein (a potent FGF21 inducer) during CR could help interpret any FGF21 or metabolic signaling changes observed in this cohort.

papers.title: Potential roles of unmeasured spectra and light’s frequency-dependent neural modulation in daylight-mediated metabolic improvements
papers.abstract: Wu et al. highlight unmeasured spectra (e.g., NIR) and light’s frequency-dependent neural modulation as key factors underlying daylight’s metabolic benefits, guiding future lighting and metabolic disease research.

papers.title: NAMing the challenges and opportunities of new approach methodologies for studying cardiovascular metabolism
papers.abstract: This letter evaluates the use of new approach methodologies (NAMs), such as human-based in vitro and in silico platforms, to reduce, refine, or replace reliance on animal testing, and to enhance our understanding of cardiovascular metabolism.

papers.title: New approach methodologies: Don’t throw the baby out with the bathwater
papers.abstract: The NIH has recently called for expanding use of new approach methodologies (NAMs) to reduce use of animals in research. Arany discusses the need not to forget the irreplaceable aspects of animal research and their fundamental importance to biomedical advancement.

papers.title: Neutrophil-mediated anti-tumoral effects of caloric restriction
authors: Chin Sern Yiong, Melissa Shu Feng Ng
papers.abstract: Caloric restriction confers several benefits in cancer, but the underlying mechanisms are not understood. In this issue of Cell Metabolism, Gao et al. highlight a newly identified neutrophil-dependent pathway modulated by caloric restriction.

papers.title: Why timing matters in MASLD: Late-day metabolic control
papers.abstract: Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD) is traditionally viewed as a static consequence of chronic caloric excess and insulin resistance. In the work previewed here, MASLD instead emerges as a disorder of time-of-day-dependent metabolic vulnerability, characterized by reduced insulin availability and worsening multisystem insulin resistance in the evening compared with the morning.

papers.title: Regulatory T cells thrive in ammonia-rich tumors
papers.abstract: In a recent issue of Cell, Gu et al. find that regulatory T (Treg) cells metabolize tumor-derived ammonia via the urea cycle and spermine synthesis, promoting immunosuppression through PPARγ-dependent oxidative phosphorylation. Inhibition of tumor glutamine metabolism reduces ammonia levels and overcomes Treg cell-mediated resistance to anti-PD-1 therapy.

papers.title: The dual role of the pentose phosphate pathway in homeostasis and regeneration
authors: Ziyu Li, Jiaqi Xu, Yi Li
papers.abstract: In a recent issue of Cell, Song et al. show that the pentose phosphate pathway (PPP) plays dual roles as a metabolic checkpoint. Spatial enrichment of PPP in peripheral nerves maintains mechanosensation homeostasis via NADPH-dependent redox balance, shifting to promote axon regeneration after injury by providing ribose-5-phosphate for RNA synthesis.

papers.title: Overcoming impasse in nutrition science
papers.abstract: Paradigm clash plays a crucial role in science by strengthening existing conceptual models or facilitating adoption of more useful ones. Here, I argue that this salutary process tends to stagnate in the field of nutrition to the detriment of both scientific advancement and public health, and I propose potential solutions.

papers.title: Suggested improvements to clarity of glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonist publications
authors: Steven B. Heymsfield, Christian Rodriguez, Shengping Yang
papers.abstract: An abundance of publications is drawing attention to the decrements in skeletal muscle mass accompanying weight loss mediated by glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists. Herein we advance three suggestions for improving the clarity of these reports that will lead to their improved interpretation by the scientific community.

papers.title: Modeling lipid homeostasis using stable isotope tracing and flux analysis
authors: Karl Wessendorf-Rodriguez, Maureen L. Ruchhoeft, Christopher W. Murray, Yale Huang, Ethan L. Ashley, Hector M. Galvez, Grace H. McGregor, Shrikaar Kambhampati, Reuben J. Shaw, Christian M. Metallo
papers.abstract: Wessendorf-Rodriguez et al. develop a pipeline for applying metabolic flux analysis to quantify lipid homeostasis. Lipid-MFA elucidates differences in synthesis and salvage fluxes in precision-cut lung slices harboring tumors with common driver mutations of lung adenocarcinoma. Application of Lipid-MFA reveals distinct trafficking of ceramide species into the sphingolipidome.

papers.title: Human MASLD is a diurnal disease driven by multisystem insulin resistance and reduced insulin availability at night
authors: Thomas Marjot, Kieran Smith, Felix Westcott, Sarah White, Elspeth Johnson, Nikola Srnic, Amy Barrett, Ellis Hall, Kate Gralton, Kaitlyn Dennis, Hamish Miller, Riccardo Pofi, Jeremy F.L. Cobbold, Rebecca Richmond, Fredrik Karpe, Ronnie Blazev, Matthew J. Watt, Benjamin L. Parker, Leanne Hodson, David W. Ray, Jeremy W. Tomlinson
papers.abstract: By studying metabolism across day and night in human participants, Marjot et al. show that MASLD is a nighttime disease driven by poor insulin action and low insulin levels. These daily patterns persist after weight loss, suggesting that nighttime metabolic dysfunction is a key driver of liver fat accumulation.

papers.title: Ketogenic diet alleviates septic lung injury via microbial gut-lung axis
authors: Mingyuan Wei, Xinzhu Yi, Zhuandi Lin, Jingjing Cai, Shanze Chen, Fang Zhou, Hanqin Cai, Xinfeng Lu, Juan Tang, Yunfeng Shi, Liping Cui, Lei Yang, Xinru Jing, Yunong Zeng, Rong Wu, Tingting Shu, Yuanyuan Li, Fangyuan Yang, Yi He, Zhibin Zhao, Yuqiong Yang, Wenjiao Zhu, Qiong Jiang, Weiwei Zhang, Xiang Tan, Zhuo Ma, Xiaoying Fan, Wei Ma, Sheng Li, Wensheng Shu, Hong Wei, Zhiming Xiang, Jack A. Gilbert, Shenhai Gong, Zhang Wang
papers.abstract: Wei et al. show that a ketogenic diet protects against sepsis-induced lung injury via a gut-lung axis. Specific gut lactobacilli convert dietary oleic acid into azelaic acid, which translocates to the lung, promotes efferocytosis via PPAR-γ-mediated expansion of MerTK+ macrophages, and correlates with improved clinical outcomes in patients.

papers.title: Metabolic stress sensing by epithelial RXRα links westernization of diet with Crohn’s disease
authors: Moritz Meyer, Felix Grabherr, Christina Plattner, Michel V. Hadjihannas, Zhigang Rao, Valentin Marteau, Víctor Alonso López-Agudelo, Julian Schwärzler, Lisa Mayr, Almina Jukic, Laura Scheffauer, Luis Zundel, Barbara Enrich, Alexandra Pfister, Anna Simonini, Christoph Grander, Richard Hilbe, David Haschka, Andreas Zollner, Kathrin Vouk, Patrizia Moser, Michael W. Hess, Niloofar Nemati, Dietmar Rieder, Felix Sommer, Philip Rosenstiel, Qitao Ran, Richard S. Blumberg, Arthur Kaser, Florian Rieder, Andreas Koeberle, Christoph Becker, Raja Atreya, Anja A. Kühl, Britta Siegmund, Andre Franke, The IBDome Consortium, Herbert Tilg, Zlatko Trajanoski, Timon E. Adolph
papers.abstract: The long-chain fatty acid receptor RXRα exhibits transcriptional activation in the intestinal epithelium of patients with Crohn’s disease (CD). Dietary polyunsaturated fatty acids induce intestinal epithelial RXRα activity in susceptible mice, which instigates chronic enteritis emanating from Paneth cells. This study reveals a cellular and molecular framework for fatty acid sensing in intestinal inflammation, which could be exploited to prevent or treat CD.

papers.title: Red blood cells serve as a primary glucose sink to improve glucose tolerance at altitude
authors: Yolanda Martí-Mateos, Zohreh Safari, Shaun Bevers, Ayush D. Midha, Will R. Flanigan, Tej Joshi, Helen Huynh, Brandon R. Desousa, Skyler Y. Blume, Alan H. Baik, Stephen Rogers, Aaron V. Issaian, Allan Doctor, Angelo D’Alessandro, Isha H. Jain
papers.abstract: Martí-Mateos et al. show that red blood cells explain improved glucose tolerance at high altitude. By manipulating red blood cell number, they determine that these cells are necessary and sufficient for hypoxic hypoglycemia. This highlights red blood cells as unappreciated glucose regulators and reveals novel strategies to treat diabetes.

papers.title: Deletion of Mfn2 in endothelial cells triggers a mitohormetic response that improves systemic metabolism and healthspan in mice
authors: Iñigo Chivite, Erika Monelli, Margalida Munar-Gelabert, Alicia G. Gómez-Valadés, Abdiel Alvarado-Diaz, Macarena Pozo, Luis Varela, Sara Ramírez, Roberta Haddad-Tóvolli, Miriam Toledo, Júlia Fos-Domènech, Francisco Díaz-Castro, Iasim Tahiri, Pau García-Ramón, Mariana Ferreira, Chloé van Gelder, Anne Abot, Óscar Osorio-Conles, José Antonio Valer, Arnaud Obri, Maria Milà-Guasch, Jorge Alvarez Luis, Pilar Villacampa, Elena Eyre, Jordi Altirriba, Sabrina Genßler, Markus Haake, Christine Schuberth-Wagner, Xavier Remesar, Antonio Zorzano, Pablo M. Garcia-Rovés, Francesc Ventura, Josep Vidal, Claude Knauf, Rubén Nogueiras, Tamas L. Horvath, Katrien De Bock, Mariona Graupera, Marc Claret
papers.abstract: Chivite et al. show that the genetic inactivation of the mitochondrial fusion protein MFN2 in the endothelium triggers a protective stress response that boosts fat burning, increases secretion of the hormone GDF15, and protects mice from obesity and age-related physical and cognitive decline.

papers.title: Microbial metabolite FAD mobilizes adipocyte lipid remodeling to enhance cancer immunotherapy efficacy
authors: Tianying Tong, Xiaowen Huang, Lingxi Li, Muni Hu, Xiaoqiang Zhu, Beiyao Zhu, Yanru Ma, Lijun Ning, Yi Jiang, Yue Zhang, Yilu Zhou, Zhenyu Wang, Jinmei Ding, Ying Zhao, Baoqin Xuan, Youwei Zhang, Xiuying Xiao, Jing-Yuan Fang, Jie Hong, Yan Yin, Fenglin Liu, Haoyan Chen
papers.abstract: Tong et al. identify a microbiota-adipose tissue axis that modulates immune checkpoint blockade efficacy in obesity. Riboflavin-producing gut bacteria elevate mesenteric adipose FAD levels, activating FADS2-mediated polyunsaturated fatty acid synthesis that enhances CD8+ T cell cytotoxicity and antitumor responses. Dietary or microbial interventions targeting this axis may offer new strategies to optimize cancer immunotherapy outcomes.

papers.title: Semaglutide ameliorates osteoarthritis progression through a weight loss-independent metabolic restoration mechanism
authors: Hongyu Qin, Jiamin Yu, Huan Yu, Chuqiao Zhou, Dongfeng Yuan, Ziling Wang, Zhenglin Zhu, Guizheng Wei, Peiyan Ou, Zhibin Li, Hua Jiang, Jie Shen, Guozhi Xiao, Xiaochun Bai, Huaiyu Wang, Huan-Tian Zhang, John R. Speakman, Di Chen, Liping Tong
papers.abstract: Qin et al. demonstrated the chondroprotective effects of semaglutide in obesity-related osteoarthritis. Mechanistically, these effects are independent of body weight loss and involve metabolic reprogramming of chondrocyte via the GLP-1R-AMPK-PFKFB3 signaling axis.

papers.title: Restricting lipid accumulation in tumor-infiltrating neutrophils mediates caloric restriction-induced anti-cancer effects
authors: Jian Gao, Wei Zhang, Qian Li, Dan Zhao, Jiayi Cai, Qing Wang, Xin Li, Tingting Liu, Jin Li, Wengan Xiao, Huimin Li, Min Du, Bing Zhang, Peiying Li, Hong Tu, Yu Gan
papers.abstract: Gao et al. reveal that caloric restriction exerts broad anti-tumor effects by reducing tumor-infiltrating neutrophils (TINs) and, importantly, inhibiting their HILPDA-mediated lipid accumulation via a blood-to-TIN IGF-1/HIF-1α/HILPDA axis, thereby suppressing tumor growth and enhancing anti-tumor immunity and anti-PD-1 efficacy by reducing TIN-derived lipid transfer to cancer and immune effector cells.

papers.title: Uridine depletion impairs CD8⁺ T cell antitumor activity through N-glycosylation
authors: Jianbiao Xiao, Zhiyang Li, Yi Ding, Kejin Zhu, Zhihao Zheng, Yaowei Zhang, Jiawen Weng, Feifei Wang, Yuqin Zhang, Sisi Zeng, Minxing Qiu, Zhaowen Zhang, Zhizhang Wang, Li Liang
papers.abstract: Xiao et al. reveal that sorting nexin 17 (SNX17) promotes the synthesis of uridine phosphorylase 1 (UPP1) in tumor cells, leading to a decrease in uridine concentration within the tumor microenvironment (TME). This reduction subsequently impairs the antitumor efficacy of CD8⁺ T cells in the TME.

papers.title: Early-activated extracellular matrix proteins shape the metabolic and spatial dynamics of the kidney fibrotic microenvironment
Obesity as a catalyst for neurodegeneration
White adipocyte thermogenesis is not an artefact but an in vivo phenomenon
PFKM governs metabolic shifts throughout skeletal muscle differentiation
Editorial Expression of Concern: The flavonoid procyanidin C1 has senotherapeutic activity and increases lifespan in mice
Fatty acids promote uncoupled respiration via ATP/ADP carriers in white adipocytes
Spatial protein gradients pattern liver metabolism
Single-cell spatial proteomics maps human liver zonation patterns and their vulnerability to disruption in tissue architecture
Increased fibroblast growth factor 21 is associated with metabolic syndrome in chronic spinal cord injury: A cross-sectional study.
papers.journal: The journal of spinal cord medicine
papers.abstract: To investigate the association between plasma fibroblast growth factor 21 (FGF21) and conventional metabolic parameters in people with chronic spinal cord injury (SCI). A cross-sectional study. An SCI-specialized rehabilitation facility in a university hospital. Fifty-six participants with chronic SCI (duration of SCI ≥ 1 year). Not applicable. After informed consent, demographic and SCI-related parameters were collected. Metabolic parameters, including anthropometric measures, body compo

papers.title: Fibroblast growth factor 21 prevents catecholaminergic arrhythmias in a mouse model of PKP2 arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy.
papers.abstract: Pathogenic variants in plakophilin-2 (PKP2) cause arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy (ACM) with intracellular calcium dysregulation as a major component of its arrhythmia phenotype. Recent adeno-associated viral (AAV) based Pkp2 (AAV-PKP2) gene therapy has shown promising results in a few different PKP2-associated ACM models. Fibroblast growth factor 21 (FGF21) has multiple cardioprotective effects and has recently emerged as a promising therapeutic agent for cardiovascular disease. To assess the eff

papers.title: Feasibility of remnant cholesterol as a therapeutic target for atherosclerotic cardio-vascular disease.
papers.abstract: Elevated plasma concentration of remnant cholesterol, the cholesterol in triglyceride-rich lipoproteins, is associated with increased risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD) in large observational epidemiological and Mendelian randomization studies. Novel drugs with pronounced remnant cholesterol-lowering effects are developed; however, their effect on risk of ASCVD remains to be documented. Observational epidemiological and Mendelian randomization studies and randomized controlle

papers.title: Genetic evidence that FGF21 signaling reduces problematic alcohol use and alcohol-related liver disease.
papers.abstract: Fibroblast growth factor 21 (FGF21) analogs are in development for metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD), but their impact on problematic alcohol use (PAU), alcohol use disorder (AUD), binge drinking, and alcohol-related liver disease (ARLD) is unknown. We leveraged GWAS data from the UK Biobank, FinnGen, Million Veterans Program, and GenomALC for PAU, AUD, binge drinking, weekly drinks, and ARLD. Our four-tier evaluation included: (1) multivariable Mendelian randomiza

papers.title: Enhanced Effects of Static Magnetic Field Combined With FGF21SS in Diabetic Mice.
papers.abstract: The increasing diabetic population, as well as the prevalence of high-fat diet-induced pre-diabetic conditions, have made it imperative to develop more effective and safe long-term prevention and treatment strategies. In recent years, both the physical method static magnetic field (SMF), and the endogenous hormone fibroblast growth factor 21 (FGF21) have shown promising treatment efficacy in type 2 diabetic animals. In this study, we further examined the effects of SMFs and FGF21SS (a thermal-st

papers.title: Acceleration of Lactate Uptake and Utilization Contributes to Neuroprotective Action of FGF21 Involved in Naturally Aging Mice.
papers.abstract: Brain aging is characterized by neuroinflammation and lactate metabolic changes. However, the functional role of FGF21 in the aging brain and its influence on lactate homeostasis remains unclear until now. In the study, male C57BL/6 mice were divided into 2-month-old (control), 20-month (aging), and FGF21-treated aging mice (FGF21). We also examined the MAPK signals and astrocyte-neuron lactate shuttle (ANLS) proteins in wild-type and hydroxycarboxylic acid receptor 1-knockout (HCA1-KO) mice wit

papers.title: Pine nut oil as a functional alternative to corn oil: Process selection, provenance profiling, and multi-omics evidence of lipid-lowering efficacy in Western-diet mice.
papers.abstract: Pine nut oil (PNO) is a candidate alternative to corn oil (CO) owing to comparable unsaturated fatty-acid profiles and enrichment in pinolenic acid (Δ5-18:3) and lipid-soluble micronutrients. We systematically compared extraction routes (solvent, supercritical CO₂, pressing), established solvent extraction as the optimal balance of yield and bioactive retention, and then characterized solvent-extracted oils from eight provenances using a weighted composite score to nominate Pinus ta

papers.title: Edible mRNA platforms: The next frontier in precision functional foods.
papers.abstract: The tendency toward functional foods is moving not toward the fixed intake of nutrient supplementation but toward dynamic biological regulation. Nanocarriers of edible mRNA are disruptive technologies, whereby foods may serve as bioreactors to induce temporary, targeted delivery of genes without posing the threat of genomic integration. The review is the summation of 156 peer-reviewed papers (2022--2025) and industrial reports to evaluate the bases of the technology, translational prospects, and

papers.title: Downregulation of Hepatic PPA1 Protects Against Obesity by Elevating FGF21 Production via eIF2α Phosphorylation.
papers.abstract: Chronic overnutrition promotes excessive hepatic triglyceride accumulation, subsequently leading to insulin resistance and systemic metabolic dysfunction. Inorganic pyrophosphatase 1 (PPA1), an enzyme that hydrolyzes inorganic pyrophosphate, plays a key role in driving synthetic biochemical reactions. Here, we identified PPA1 as a novel regulator of systemic energy expenditure that functions by controlling hepatic production of fibroblast growth factor 21 (FGF21). FGF21 is a hormone predominantl

papers.title: Molecular hydrogen mitigates acetaminophen-induced liver injury and enhances the effects of N-acetylcysteine in diabetic mice.
papers.abstract: Acetaminophen (APAP) overdose induces severe liver injury, especially in diabetic patients. N-acetylcysteine (NAC) is commonly used as an approved antidote for APAP toxicity; however, its narrow therapeutic window limits its clinical utility. This study investigated the protective effects of molecular hydrogen (H₂) against APAP-induced hepatotoxicity in diabetic mice. Diabetic db/db mice were provided with hydrogen-dissolved water (H₂-water) for two weeks prior to APAP administrati

papers.title: Klotho protein and insulin resistance.
papers.journal: Clinica chimica acta; international journal of clinical chemistry
papers.abstract: Klotho was initially identified as an anti-ageing protein expressed mainly in renal distal tubular cells and the choroid plexus. The Klotho family comprises several members, including α-Klotho (αKl), β-Klotho (βKl), Klotho-LPH related protein (KLPH; lactase-phlorizin hydrolase), and Klotho-related protein (KlrP), which perform a multitude of functions related to various physiological processes. The effects of Klotho on various ageing-related and renal diseases, such as it

papers.title: Using gas exchange measurements to monitor growth, energy expenditure, and body composition of black soldier fly larvae (Hermetia illucens) on diets varying in protein-to-carbohydrate ratio.
papers.abstract: Optimizing black soldier fly larvae (BSFL) production requires a better understanding of how diet composition shapes growth, nutrient allocation, and metabolic efficiency. This study investigated whether respiratory measurements could serve as predictive, non-invasive tools for BSFL performance on diets differing in protein-to-carbohydrate (P:C) ratio. Larvae were reared for seven days on four experimental diets, with growth, survival, gas exchange (O2 consumption, CO2 production, respiratory ex

papers.title: A Rare Presentation of Right-Sided Pleural Effusion in Decompensated Chronic Liver Disease Secondary to Hepatitis C Infection: A Case Report.
papers.abstract: Hepatic hydrothorax (HH) is an infrequent and clinically important complication of decompensated liver cirrhosis. It occurs in around 5%-10% of affected people. Hepatic hydrothorax is basically a transudative pleural effusion, usually right-sided and commonly without evidence of primary cardiopulmonary or renal pathology. We present the case of a 73-year-old female patient with poorly monitored hepatitis C virus (HCV) infection presenting to the hospital with progressive abdominal distension, sh

papers.title: Foods for special medical purposes for the dietary therapy of rare diseases: Current status and future prospects.
papers.abstract: Foods for special medical purposes (FSMPs) is a type of products that provides targeted nutritional support for specific diseases or physiological conditions. Compared with conventional dietary therapy, FSMPs are more targeted, safer, and applicable in clinic. When FSMPs are used to treat rare diseases, their core principle is to bypass or alleviate metabolic disorders. With the increasing recognition of clinical treatment effectiveness and the growing demand from patients, the types and market

papers.title: Effect of Dietary Electrolyte Balance in Low-Protein Diet on Intestinal Barrier, Mitochondrial Function and Carcass Quality of Growing Pigs.
papers.journal: Journal of animal physiology and animal nutrition
papers.abstract: In a low-protein diet, dietary electrolyte balance may be disrupted due to the reduction in the amount of soybean meal used and supplementation with lysine hydrochloride. The objective of this experiment was to evaluate the effects of dEB on intestinal barrier, mitochondrial function and carcass quality of growing pigs fed a low-protein diet. A total of 144 pigs (initial body weight 25.45 ± 0.36kg) were allotted to the control group with a crude protein (CP) level of 15.7% (CO

papers.title: Nitrogen-protected thermal induction combined with Microbial Transglutaminase (MTG) cross-linking enhances functional properties and gel structure of Soy Protein Isolate relevant to gel-based feed formulation.
papers.abstract: Protein-based gel matrices are increasingly explored for feed applications requiring soft or semi-solid formulations, but achieving strong gelation along with high water- and oil-holding capacities at low protein concentrations remains challenging. In this study, soy protein isolate (SPI) was modified via nitrogen-protected thermal induction followed by microbial transglutaminase (MTG) cross-linking to enhance its suitability as a gel-feed matrix. Single-factor and orthogonal experimental design

papers.title: Effects of reduced metabolizable protein diets supplemented with rumen-protected His under adequate Met and Lys supply on production, nutrient digestibility, N utilization, and manure ammonia emissions in lactating cows.
papers.abstract: Balancing for limiting AA may prevent reduced production when deficient MP is provided. The objective of the study was to determine the effects of rumen-protected (RP) His supplementation at different levels of MP when Lys and Met supplies meet the requirements. Sixty lactating cows (mean ± SD; parity, 2.47 ± 1.346; DIM, 112 ± 27; milk yield, 50 ± 8 kg/d) were used in a randomized complete block design and allocated to one of the following diets: an MP-adequate diet meeting t

papers.title: Efimosfermin alfa (BOS-580) once per month in people with metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis with F2 or F3 fibrosis: results from a 24-week, randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled, phase 2 trial.
papers.abstract: Efimosfermin alfa (BOS-580) is an FGF21 analogue dosed once monthly by subcutaneous injection. In this 24-week phase 2 RCT in patients with biopsy-confirmed MASH (F2/F3 fibrosis), efimosfermin was well tolerated with mostly mild-to-moderate GI side effects. No deaths or grade 3+ adverse events were observed. Results support continued development of monthly FGF21 analogue therapy for MASH-related fibrosis.

papers.title: Rapid Remodeling of the Human Gut Microbiome in Response to Short-Term Animal Product Restriction and Associations with Host Molecular Phenotypes.
papers.abstract: Multi-omics profiling (16S rRNA, metabolomics, proteomics) of 200 individuals alternating between omnivory and animal product restriction for religious reasons. Dietary restriction reduced microbial diversity and shifted one-third of bacterial genera. A diet-responsive module associated Negativibacillus with FGF21 and intermediate-density lipoproteins, suggesting FGF21 as a circulating mediator linking gut microbiome remodeling to host metabolic responses during protein/animal product restriction.

papers.title: Decoding the mechanistic basis of liver-muscle communication in health and disease.
papers.abstract: Comprehensive review of liver-skeletal muscle crosstalk via inflammatory mediators (TNF-α, IL-6), metabolic regulators (mTOR, AMPK), hepatokines, and myokines. FGF21 is mentioned as part of liver-heart crosstalk alongside IL-6/STAT3 signaling. Broad review with FGF21 as a peripheral reference point rather than a primary focus.
